source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 4.8 Special Points (pp. 64-68)

questions.q: What does the tee command do?
options: Sends a command's output to a file AND keeps it displayed on screen
Splits a file into two halves
questions.why: tee, combined with a pipe, writes the incoming output to a file while still showing it on screen; -a appends instead of overwriting.

questions.q: List '/' in long format and, using tee, save the output to the file fic while still showing it on screen.
questions.why: The pipe feeds ls's output to tee, which both writes fic and prints to the terminal.

questions.q: List the aliases defined for the current shell session.
questions.why: alias with no argument lists the current session's aliases (ls, ll, grep colouring, etc.).

questions.q: Create an alias named ll that runs 'ls -l'.
questions.why: alias name='command' defines a shortcut; it lasts only for the session unless saved to a config file.

questions.q: Where must an alias be defined to make it PERMANENT?
options: In ~/.bashrc for one user, or /etc/bashrc for all users
questions.why: Session aliases vanish at logout; put them in .bashrc (per user) or /etc/bashrc (all users) to persist them.

questions.q: Delete the single alias named ll.
questions.why: unalias removes an alias; 'unalias -a' would remove them all.

questions.q: How do you run a command ONCE while ignoring its alias, without deleting the alias?
options: Prefix it with a backslash, e.g. \ls
Prefix it with a dot, e.g. .ls
Suffix it with an ampersand, e.g. ls&
Type it in capitals, e.g. LS
questions.why: A leading backslash (\ls) disables the alias for that one invocation, running the real command.

questions.q: In 'ls /; cd /home; ls -lia; cd /', what does the ; character do?
options: It strings the commands so they run sequentially, in order, on one line
It runs the commands in parallel
It pipes each command's output to the next
It comments out the rest of the line
questions.why: The ; separator chains commands to run one after another in the order entered when you press Enter.
